evals.prompt: I have a Shopify skincare brand, about 4,000 customers total, been around 2 years. I know our repeat purchase rate is somewhere around 25% which feels low. I don't really do any segmentation right now — everyone gets the same emails. I can pull an order export from Shopify if needed. I want to understand who my customers actually are and what to do differently for each group. Like, who are my VIPs, who's about to churn, who only bought once and never came back. And then tell me what to actually DO with each segment — specific emails, offers, that kind of thing.
evals.expected_output: RFM-based segmentation with 5-7 named segments sized for a 4,000 customer base, lifecycle stage overlay, VIP identification (top 20% / Pareto analysis), specific marketing actions per segment (not just labels), email tactic recommendations including welcome series for new and win-back for at-risk, measurement plan with expected lift targets.
expectations: Defines at least 5 distinct customer segments with names, not just RFM scores
Estimates segment sizes as percentages appropriate for a 4,000 customer base
Includes lifecycle stages (new, active, at-risk, lapsed/churned)
Identifies VIP/top customers and quantifies their revenue share (Pareto)
Provides specific marketing actions for each segment, not just descriptions
Recommends a welcome series for new/one-time buyers to drive second purchase
Recommends a win-back flow for at-risk customers with specific offer guidance
Notes that 25% repeat rate is low for skincare and frames segmentation as a path to improve it

evals.prompt: ok so I sell pet food and treats on shopify and amazon. we have maybe 8,000 customers across both channels. the business is very repeat-heavy — our best customers order every 3-4 weeks like clockwork. But I have a feeling we're losing a lot of people after their first or second order and I don't know why. I also want to figure out which customers buy only treats vs only food vs both, because I think there's a big cross-sell opportunity. I have Klaviyo but honestly I'm just blasting the whole list with the same newsletter. Can you help me build proper segments?
evals.expected_output: Segmentation tailored to a high-frequency consumable (3-4 week cycle), lifecycle thresholds adjusted for pet food (shorter than apparel), product affinity analysis (food-only vs treats-only vs both), cross-sell strategy for single-category buyers, Klaviyo-specific segment setup guidance, focus on the first-to-second purchase drop-off.
expectations: Adjusts lifecycle thresholds for pet food's 3-4 week repurchase cycle (not generic 90-day)
Addresses the first-to-second purchase drop-off as a specific problem to solve
Includes product affinity segmentation (food-only, treats-only, both)
Recommends cross-sell tactics for single-category buyers
Mentions or accounts for the dual-channel complexity (Shopify + Amazon)
Provides Klaviyo-compatible segment definitions or rules
Recommends stopping the 'blast the whole list' approach with specific alternatives
Includes at least one replenishment/reminder flow suggestion based on the 3-4 week cycle

evals.prompt: I run a DTC fashion brand — womenswear, $45-$120 price range. We've been growing fast but I realized I have no idea who my actual best customers are vs who just bought once during a sale and never came back. Our email list is 12,000 people but open rates have been dropping. I think we're emailing too many people who don't care anymore. I want to: 1) figure out who my real VIPs are and treat them better, 2) identify who's about to churn so I can try to save them, 3) clean up the list by suppressing people who are truly gone. I don't have a CSV ready but I can describe the business. Help me build the segments and tell me what to do with each one.
evals.expected_output: Works without CSV data using the described business context, defines RFM segments for fashion (longer cycle than consumables), VIP identification criteria, at-risk and churned definitions, list hygiene / sunset strategy for hibernating segment, email engagement-based suppression guidance, segment-specific marketing tactics.
expectations: Works with described data rather than requiring a CSV
Defines segments appropriate for fashion's purchase cycle (longer than consumables)
Identifies VIP criteria and recommends specific VIP treatment tactics
Defines at-risk segment with clear recency thresholds for fashion
Includes a list-cleaning / sunset strategy for truly churned subscribers
Addresses declining open rates as partly a segmentation and suppression problem
Distinguishes between sale-driven one-time buyers and genuine new customers
Provides at least 5 actionable segments with marketing tactics for each
